exemplar.title:

Adoption Rate of Battery Electric Vehicles in the state of Maharashtra?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Precisely identifies the Maharashtra EV Policy 2021 and maintains clear, consistent focus throughout.
Research question is narrowly framed, clearly stated, and effectively connected to each section.
Methodology outlines an appropriate mixed-methods design with justified source selection and analytical tools.
weaknesses: Minor wording slip (âboatsâ instead of âboastsâ) affects academic precision.
Regression analysis is based on only four annual observations, limiting robustness.
Survey sampling strategy is convenience-based and could benefit from clearer justification of representativeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates topic, purpose and research question at a high level (band 5-6) and employs an appropriate mixed-methods approach. Minor stylistic slips and some methodological limitations prevent full marks, so a score of 5/6 accurately reflects strengths in clarity and planning balanced against those weaknesses.

strengths: Integrates official statistics, peer-reviewed studies and policy documents effectively.
Demonstrates clear, coherent knowledge of economic theory (externalities, Pigouvian subsidies, indifference curves).
Uses economic terminology accurately and consistently, with well-labelled diagrams and tables.
weaknesses: Some data (e.g. utility ratings) are treated uncritically.
Duplicate citations appear in the reference list.
Minor typographical slip (MSP vs MSB in one figure key).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Source material is applied appropriately and theory is used coherently (bands 3-4 and 5-6), with generally accurate terminology. Small lapses in citation management and critical application of certain data place this work just below perfection, warranting a 5/6.

strengths: Research draws on a substantial, relevant evidence base (secondary data, policy documents, survey).
Analysis is clear, with supported conclusions from regression, percentage-change calculations and welfare diagrams.
Discussion produces logical recommendations and recognises key policy impacts and limitations.
weaknesses: Time-series regression with only four data points limits confidence and projection accuracy.
Evaluation stops short of deeply critical appraisal of methodological assumptions.
Qualitative survey analysis plan is omitted; coding or thematic approach is not explained.
Unrealistic assumption of 100% socially optimal EV quantity is not sufficiently challenged.
Standard errors or confidence intervals for regression coefficients are not presented.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates consistently appropriate and relevant research (band 7-9) and clear analysis (band 4-6), with a reasoned argument (band 4-6). However, limitations in data depth, methodological critique and statistical rigour prevent full marks. A score of 11/12 reflects strong critical thinking tempered by these shortcomings.

strengths: Follows IB EE structure with all required elements (title page, abstract, TOC, sections, references, appendices).
Tables, figures and equations are neatly embedded; word count and title are clearly presented.
Consistent heading hierarchy and overall formatting support readability.
weaknesses: Research question is split across headers, hindering readability.
Duplicate references and minor line-break issues in Harvard citations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the conventions of the EE format (band 3-4) with clear structure and formatting. Minor pagination and citation issues prevent a perfect score, so a 3/4 appropriately reflects its quality.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is quoted verbatim, is narrowly framed (âto what extent did â¦ impact the adoption rate of BEVs in Maharashtra?â), and is repeatedly referenced in the introduction, methodology and conclusion.  It is clearly focused on one policy, one good and one geographical area, which provides a direct line of enquiry that can be investigated with available data.  Minor stylistic redundancies (split lines in the heading) do not undermine clarity, so performance fits the 5-6 band.

The student identifies a precise policy (Maharashtra EV Policy 2021) and frames the study as an evaluation of its effectiveness in correcting a market failure.  The title, abstractâstyle opening and introductory section all communicate the topic clearly, while the merit-good rationale and externalities are explicitly linked to Economics.  Although there are minor wording slips (e.g. âboatsâ instead of âboastsâ), the purpose and focus remain consistently appropriate and are sustained throughout the table of contents and section headings.  Hence the descriptors for the 5-6 band are largely met.

The essay draws extensively on official statistics, peer-reviewed studies and policy documents, and integrates them in tables, figures and in-text discussion.  Source use is mostly relevant, but some data (e.g. utility ratings) are treated uncritically and duplicate citations appear in the reference list, indicating only âmostlyâ appropriate application.  This places the work solidly in the 3-4 band, at its top end.

The essay follows the IB EE structureâtitle page, abstract-style introduction, contents page, numbered sections, conclusion, works cited and appendices.  All required elements are present and logically ordered.  Minor pagination inconsistencies (some section breaks labelled only by numbers) prevent a flawless rating, so a score of 3 within the top band is appropriate.

Tables, figures and equations are neatly embedded; headings use consistent hierarchy; word count and title are clearly shown.  Harvard-style references are provided, though a few duplicates and line breaks appear.  Overall formatting supports readability and complies with EE conventions, meriting placement in the higher band with a score of 3.

The discussion weighs price incentives against non-price barriers, critiques the policy target versus projected 5.7 % outcome, and recognises data limitations (COVID shock, small sample).  Recommendations (larger subsidies, infrastructure, ICE disincentives) flow logically from findings.  Evaluation is reasoned but not deeply critical of methodological weaknesses or alternative explanations, so it corresponds to âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ (band 4-6, score 5).

Analysis includes percentage-change calculations, a linear regression with interpretation of coefficient and RÂ², and application of perfect-substitutes utility to derive optimal bundles and percentage change post-subsidy.  Diagrams illustrate welfare loss before and after intervention.  Conclusions are generally supported, but the regressionâs four data points and some simplifying assumptions weaken rigour.  Hence analysis is âclear with mostly supported conclusionsâ, fitting the middle band (4-6) with a score of 5.

The student demonstrates coherent knowledge of externalities, Pigouvian subsidies, indifference curves and perfect-substitutes utility, applying them correctly to the EV context.  Definitions are accurate, diagrams are labelled and explained, and limitations of linear regressions are acknowledged.  Occasional over-statement of âsocially optimal 100 %â and treatment of quantities as percentages show minor lapses, but overall the knowledge is clear and logically organised, meeting the 5-6 band (score 5).

The secondary data series, government policy documents and 150-response survey together constitute a substantial body of evidence that is directly relevant to the RQ.  Although time-series depth is limited, most research chosen clearly links to adoption rates and consumer behaviour, showing purposeful selection.  This aligns with âmajority of research is appropriate and clearly relevantâ, placing the work in the 7-9 band at its lower end.

A mixed-methods design is outlined: (i) secondary data from government registration databases analysed with linear regression, and (ii) a 150-respondent survey providing quantitative ratings and two qualitative questions.  Rationale for source choice (reliability, representativeness of urban consumers) is given and variables, sample size and analytical tools are explained.  While the regression rests on only four annual observations and the sampling strategy is somewhat convenience-based, the range and selection evidence are still âappropriateâ and âinformedâ, meeting the upper-band description.

Economic terminologyâMSB, MPC, welfare loss, indifference curves, marginal utility per rupee, RÂ², etc.âis used consistently and accurately.  Symbols are defined, and the distinction between market and socially optimal outcomes is properly expressed.  A few typographical slips (MSB vs MSP in figure key) do not materially affect accuracy; thus the descriptors for accurate and consistent terminology are satisfied (band 5-6, score 5).

annotations.comment: The paragraph cuts off mid-sentence at the page break. Ensure the full explanation of factors is included.
Methodology outlines a mixed-methods approach with justified source selection, demonstrating structured planning.
The title page prominently displays the investigation title and âEXTENDED ESSAYâ heading, fulfilling report identification requirements.
The word count is clearly shown beneath the subject line, meeting the formatting requirement for Extended Essays.
The research question is split across multiple headers and text blocks, hindering readability. Consider consolidating it into one continuous element.
The table of contents is comprehensive and lists all major and sub-sections with corresponding pages, facilitating clear navigation.
Indentation for sub-sections in the contents page is inconsistent, reducing visual hierarchy. Align sub-section entries uniformly to improve clarity.
Typographical slip: âboatsâ in the introduction should read âboasts.â Correct this to maintain academic precision.
Strong integration of WHO data linking transport emissions to mortality provides compelling context for the policy need.
Table 1 effectively summarizes the MH-EV Policyâs key components and subsidy criteria, aiding reader comprehension.
Consider adding a source citation in the table caption for subsidy cap figures to reinforce data credibility.
In Table 1, line breaks and punctuation are inconsistent between cells. Standardize formatting for professional presentation.
The significance section clearly links positive externalities and Pigouvian subsidies to the research question, reinforcing focus.
Label the shifted supply and demand curves directly on Figure 2 to improve clarity of post-policy effects.
Comparative examples from Australia, New Zealand, and the USA contextualize Maharashtraâs policy effectively.
Inline notation $$\bar{\xi}1.5 lakh$$ is misformatted; ensure consistent use of LaTeX for parameters and subscripts.
Figure 3âs depiction of perfect-substitutes indifference curves aligns well with utility theory concepts.
Detail the sampling strategy (e.g., random, convenience) for the survey to clarify representativeness and reduce potential bias.
The mixed-method rationale is well stated, linking consumer decision-making to policy objectives.
The method omits the analysis plan for qualitative survey responses. Explain coding or thematic analysis to strengthen methodology.
The framework correctly classifies BEVs as merit goods and explains market failure using relevant theory.
Illustrate numerical values for MSC and MSB to quantify the external benefit gap rather than relying solely on the diagram.
Asserting a 100 % socially optimal EV quantity is unrealistic. Discuss a more plausible optimal level based on data or theory.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes welfare loss and externality gap, reinforcing theoretical arguments.
The â*diagram is not to scaleâ note is helpful but discuss how scaling differences might affect interpretation of curve shifts.
Expand the discussion on how perfect substitutes assumptions may limit real-world applicability for EV and ICE choices.
Table 2âs use of official registration data demonstrates strong source application and relevance to the research question.
Discuss the implications of relying on only four annual observations for regression accuracy and projection confidence.
The regression equation and projection are clearly shown using $$y = 1.113x - 2248.1$$, aiding transparency of analysis.
Include standard errors or confidence intervals for regression coefficients to assess the reliability of the 2025 projection.
The conclusion that the policy alone is largely ineffective could be balanced by acknowledging incremental adoption benefits.
Acknowledging data limitations such as COVID-19âs impact demonstrates critical evaluation of research reliability.
